content.problemStatement:
You are given three sequences A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​, B1,B2,…,BMB_1, B_2, \ldots, B_MB1​,B2​,…,BM​ and C1,C2,…,CKC_1, C_2, \ldots, C_KC1​,C2​,…,CK​. Each sequence consists of pairwise distinct integers. You need to find how many integers there are that appear in all three of the given sequences.


Input Format


The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.




The first line of each test case contains three space-separated integers NNN, MMM and KKK.




The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​.




The third line contains MMM space-separated integers B1,B2,…,BMB_1, B_2, \ldots, B_MB1​,B2​,…,BM​.




The fourth line contains KKK space-separated integers C1,C2,…,CKC_1, C_2, \ldots, C_KC1​,C2​,…,CK​.





Output Format

For each test case, print a single line containing one integer ― the number of integers that appear in all three sequences.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 10^5
Sum of M over all test cases ≤ 10^5
Sum of K over all test cases ≤ 10^5
All numbers in a single sequence are pairwise distinct
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case:
- The first line contains three integers N, M and K.
- The second line contains N distinct integers A1 … AN.
- The third line contains M distinct integers B1 … BM.
- The fourth line contains K distinct integers C1 … CK.
All integers are between 1 and 10^9 inclusive.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing one integer – the count of numbers that appear in all three sequences.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Case 1: 2 and 5 appear in all three arrays. Case 2: no common element. Case 3: 1, 3 and 4 appear in all three arrays.
Only number 3 is present in all three sequences.

solution.approach: For each test case we need the size of the intersection of three sets. Because the elements inside each sequence are distinct we can treat each sequence as a set. We store the elements of the first sequence in a hash map (or unordered_map) with a counter initialized to 1. While reading the second sequence we increase the counter for elements already present. The same is done for the third sequence. Finally we iterate over the map and count how many keys have a counter equal to 3 – those are present in all three sequences. This runs in O(N+M+K) time and O(N+M+K) extra memory. In languages without a built‑in hash map (plain C) we sort the three arrays and use a three‑pointer technique to count common elements, which also respects the same asymptotic bounds.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N + M + K) per test case
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N + M + K) for the hash map (or O(N + M + K) for the three sorted arrays in C)
